title: Equity-Based Mortgages — When Equity Outweighs Income
metaTitle: Equity-Based Mortgage in BC — Stated-Income Options
metaDescription: Equity-based mortgages for borrowers with strong equity but limited verifiable income — retirees, self-employed and high-net-worth clients in Greater Vancouver.
intro: When traditional income documentation doesn't tell your story, equity does. Equity-based mortgages weigh the strength of your property and down payment more heavily than your line-by-line income.

body.heading: Who equity-based mortgages help
paragraphs: Three groups benefit most: retirees with significant home equity but limited employment income, self-employed borrowers whose tax-optimized income looks small on paper, and high-net-worth borrowers whose wealth is in investments and corporate accounts rather than personal T4s.

Equity-based products typically require 20–35% equity (or 20–35% down on a purchase), accept stated-income or asset-based qualification, and price at 0.5–1.5% above standard A-lender rates. They're a sensible bridge for clients who don't fit traditional underwriting today but expect to graduate back to A-lender rates within 2–5 years.

body.heading: B-lenders and credit unions
paragraphs: Most equity-based mortgages come from B-lenders (Home Trust, Equitable Bank, MCAP B-side) and credit unions (Vancity, Coast Capital, BlueShore, Prospera in BC). Credit unions are particularly flexible because they're provincially regulated and not subject to federal stress-test rules — they can qualify on the contract rate alone for some files.

whoItsFor: Are retired with significant home equity but limited fixed income
Have wealth held in corporate or investment accounts
Have temporary income disruption you can't document conventionally

faqs.question: Are equity-based mortgages more expensive?
faqs.answer: Yes — typically 0.5–1.5% above prime A-lender rates. Use them as a bridge to a better situation, not as a permanent home for your mortgage.

faqs.question: Can I move back to an A-lender later?
faqs.answer: Often yes. After 1–2 years of clean payments and improved or documented income, most clients can transfer to A-lender pricing at renewal.

faqs.question: What's the maximum loan-to-value?
faqs.answer: Generally 65–80% LTV depending on the lender and your file. Stronger files with provable down payment can push higher; thinner files cap lower.
